content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 26, 1990

Bob Allen
City Attorney's Office
City of Whittier
13230 Penn Street
Whittier, CA 90602-1772

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-701

Dear Mr. Allen:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice with
respect to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act.1/ In our telephone conversation of November 16, 1990,
you stated that you were requesting formal advice on behalf of
Whittier City Councilmember Robert Henderson. (See, Regulation
18329, copy enclosed.)

QUESTION

May Councilmember Henderson participate in a city council
decision concerning the award of a contract to Specialty Vehicles,
which was the low bidder on the contract, where the Specialty Vehicles
will be represented by an employee, Ms. Nancy Munoz, who has been
a source of income to Councilmember Henderson?

CONCLUSION

If the governmental decision will affect Ms. Munoz's salary
or other income or assets by $1,000 or more, Councilmember
Henderson must disqualify himself from participating in the
decision. However, if the only financial effect of the decision
will be on Specialty Vehicles, the councilmember will not have a
conflict of interest with respect to the decision.

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory refer-
ences are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Com-
mission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-90-701
Page 2


FACTS

Robert Henderson is a city councilmember in the city of
Whittier. Councilmember Henderson is also a life insurance agent
in the city. In the course of doing business in the jurisdiction,
the councilmember received income of $250 from Ms. Nancy Munoz.

The city council is currently considering the award of a
contract to Specialty Vehicles, the low bidder, for the city's
purchase of five trolleys. Ms. Munoz is employed by Specialty
Vehicles and will be representing Specialty Vehicles before the
city council with respect to the trolley contract. You stated in
your supplemental materials that Ms. Munoz has no ownership
interest in Specialty Vehicles, and you do not know whether she
will receive a commission if the contract is approved. You also
stated the city council is required by law to accept the lowest
qualified bidder.

You have asked whether Councilmember Henderson therefore has
a conflict of interest which will require him to disqualify
himself from participating in decisions concerning the trolley
contract.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. A "public official" is
defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700 (copy enclosed) as
every natural person who is a member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency. This definition
would include a member of the Whittier City Council.

Your supplemental materials dated November 16, 1990, state
that the city council as a matter of law must accept the lowest
bid. If the vote is purely ministerial, such that the city
council has no discretion with respect to the terms of the
contract or the acceptance of the low bidder for the contract, the
decision may not be considered a governmental decision which could
lead to a conflict of interest under the Act. (Regulation
18700(d), copy enclosed.) However, we do not have sufficient
information about the legal requirements concerning the bid to
reach a conclusion on this point. Consequently, I have enclosed
the following letters on ministerial decisions for your
information: Cano Advice Letter, No. I-90-190; Klein Advice
Letter, No. A-84-152; Sperry Advice Letter, No. A-83-235.

Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable

Our File No. A-90-701
Page 3


that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

     (c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made.

     Any person or business which has made any payment to an
official in the past 12 months is a source of income to the
official for the purposes of Section 87103. Thus, purchasers of
insurance policies from the Councilmember Henderson are sources of
income to the councilmember and will continue to be potentially
disqualifying economic interests for 12 months after receipt of
the income.27 Pursuant to Section 87103, Councilmember Henderson
may not participate in any governmental decision that will
reasonably foreseeably have a material financial effect on an
economic interest.

     Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198,
copy enclosed.)

Materiality

     In addition, the effect on the councilmember's source of
income must be material in order to require disqualification. The
Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine whether
an effect is material, depending on the specific circumstances of
each decision. For example, where a source of income is directly
before the city council, as an applicant or the subject of the

___________________
27/ Regulation 18704.3 (copy enclosed) provides guidelines for the
attribution of commission income for conflict-of-interest
purposes. Regulation 18704.3 provides that for an insurance
broker or agent, all of the following are sources of income in a
given sale: (a) The insurance company providing the policy;
(b) The person purchasing the policy; and (c) The brokerage
firm, agency, company, or other business entity through which the
broker or agent conducts business. Moreover, for purposes of
determining whether disqualification is required, the full gross
value of any commission income for a specific transaction shall be
attributed to each source of income in that sale or transaction.

Our File No. A-90-701
Page 4


decision, Regulation 18702.1(a) (copy enclosed) provides that the
effect of the decision on the source of income is deemed material
and disqualification is required.3/ (Combs Advice Letter,
No. A-89-177, copy enclosed.)

According to your facts Ms. Munoz is not directly before the
city council, but is only there in a representative capacity.
Where a source of income appears in a representative capacity, we
have advised that the person is not directly involved in a
decision. (Gill Advice Letter, No. A-85-252, copy enclosed.)
Thus, only Specialty Vehicles is directly involved in the contract
decision, and since Specialty Vehicles has not been a source of
income to the councilmember, their involvement does not result in
a conflict of interest.

However, even where a source of income is not directly before
the official's agency, disqualification is still required if the
source of income will be indirectly materially affected by the
decision. Regulation 18702.6 (copy enclosed) provides:

The effect of a decision is material as to an
individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
official if any of the following applies:

(a) The decision will affect the
individual's income, investments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real property) by $1,000 or more;
or

(b) The decision will affect the
individual's real property interest in a man-
ner that is considered material under Section
18702.3 or Section 18702.4.4/

Consequently, if the decision will affect Ms. Munoz's salary
or other interests by $1,000 or more, Councilmember Henderson may
not participate in the decision. For example, if Ms. Munoz will
receive a commission as a result of approval of the contract of
$1,000 or more, the effect of the decision on Ms. Munoz will be

_____________________
3/ A source of income is directly before the city council when
the source initiates the proceeding by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request, or is a named party in, or the
subject of, the proceeding. A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or
business entity. (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

4/ Regulations 18702.3 and 18702.4 have been enclosed for future
reference.

Our File No. A-90-701
Page 5


material and the councilmember may not participate.  However, if
the only effect will be on Specialty Vehicles, the councilmember
will not have a conflict of interest.  (Harron Advice Letter,
No. A-84-288, copy enclosed.)

Finally, even if Councilmember Henderson determines that he
has a financial interest that will be materially affected by the
decision, he may still participate in the decision if the effect
on the councilmember's financial interest is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.  For the "public
generally" exception to apply, the decision must affect the source
of income in substantially the same manner as it will affect a
significant segment of Whittier. (Regulation 18703, copy
enclosed.)   Since any effect on Ms. Munoz that will arise from
the transaction will be unique to her, the public generally
exception would not apply under these facts.

Should you have any further questions regarding this matter
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
     Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa

Enclosures
